content.text: Why Structured Reviews Are the Forcing Function

Cost data without a review cadence sits unread. Teams optimise GenAI spending when they are accountable for it in a regular, structured meeting — not when a dashboard exists somewhere. A monthly 30-minute team FinOps review converts cost visibility into action by creating a repeating cycle: report → discuss → assign → act → verify. Without the cycle, spending drifts upward unchecked until an invoice surprise forces an emergency response.

Every decision that comes out of a review meeting must be captured as a structured action item with an owner, due date, and measurable success metric. Vague agreements ('look into the cost spike') rot without follow-through. The success metric makes verification objective at the next review.

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from datetime import date
from enum import Enum
from typing import Optional

class ActionCategory(Enum):
    OPTIMISE       = "optimise"       # reduce cost without sacrificing quality
    INVESTIGATE    = "investigate"    # understand an anomaly before acting
    BUDGET_ADJUST  = "budget_adjust"  # request a budget change through governance
    ESCALATE       = "escalate"       # needs approval above team lead level
    TRACK          = "track"          # no action needed yet — monitor for 30 days

class ActionStatus(Enum):
    OPEN       = "open"
    IN_PROGRESS= "in_progress"
    DONE       = "done"
    OVERDUE    = "overdue"
    CANCELLED  = "cancelled"

@dataclass
class CostReviewActionItem:
    action_id: str
    team: str
    title: str
    description: str
    category: ActionCategory
    owner: str               # name of the person, not a team
    due_date: date
    created_date: date
    success_metric: str      # measurable outcome e.g. "P95 cost/conv < $0.25 by next review"
    status: ActionStatus = ActionStatus.OPEN
    closed_date: Optional[date] = None
    cost_impact_usd: Optional[float] = None   # actual saving after closure
    notes: str = ""

    @property
    def is_overdue(self) -> bool:
        return (
            self.status in (ActionStatus.OPEN, ActionStatus.IN_PROGRESS)
            and date.today() > self.due_date
        )

The five categories map to different timelines: INVESTIGATE items are due in 7–10 days (fast root-cause turnaround); OPTIMISE items are due in 14–30 days (implementation plus verification); BUDGET_ADJUST and ESCALATE items follow the governance calendar; TRACK items have no due date.

The report is assembled from the data already in the FinOps pipeline: spend aggregated from the conversation cost table, unit metrics from the unit economics report, and open action items from the action tracker. The report is emailed two days before the meeting so attendees arrive prepared.

The 30-minute meeting has a fixed structure. Deviating from it causes the meeting to run over or skip the most important part (new action assignment). The agenda is generated from the report so it is always specific to this team and this period, not a generic template.

def render_meeting_agenda(report: TeamCostReport) -> str:
    overdue = [a for a in report.open_actions if a.is_overdue]
    return f"""
TEAM FINOPS REVIEW — {report.team.upper()} | {report.period}
Duration: 30 minutes
Required attendees: Team lead, FinOps partner, 1–2 feature engineers

AGENDA
------
[0:00 – 0:05]  Previous action items ({len(report.open_actions)} open, {len(overdue)} overdue)
[0:05 – 0:15]  Spend walk:
                 • Budget: ${report.budget_usd:,.0f} | Actual: ${report.actual_spend_usd:,.0f} ({report.actual_spend_usd/report.budget_usd:.0%})
                 • MoM change: {report.mom_change_pct:+.1%}
                 • Top features by spend: {", ".join(list(report.spend_by_feature.keys())[:3])}
[0:15 – 0:25]  Anomaly deep-dive:
                 • Active alerts: {len(report.alerts)}
                 • Root cause discussion for each WARN/CRITICAL
[0:25 – 0:30]  New action items — assign owner + due date + success metric

PRE-READ (sent 2 days before):
  - This agenda + cost report attachment
  - Open action item list
  - Unit economics snapshot (cost/conv, LLM ratio, GM%)
"""

Action items from each review are persisted in a database. Between reviews, automated reminders fire three days before due dates and immediately when items become overdue. The FinOps partner reviews the overdue list weekly and escalates items unactioned after 10 days.

import sqlite3
from datetime import date, timedelta

def create_action_schema(db: sqlite3.Connection) -> None:
    db.executescript("""        CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS cost_review_actions (
            action_id     TEXT PRIMARY KEY,
            team          TEXT NOT NULL,
            title         TEXT NOT NULL,
            description   TEXT,
            category      TEXT NOT NULL,
            owner         TEXT NOT NULL,
            due_date      TEXT NOT NULL,
            created_date  TEXT NOT NULL,
            success_metric TEXT,
            status        TEXT NOT NULL DEFAULT 'open',
            closed_date   TEXT,
            cost_impact_usd REAL,
            notes         TEXT
        );
        CREATE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS idx_actions_team_status
            ON cost_review_actions(team, status);
    """)
    db.commit()

def get_overdue_actions(db: sqlite3.Connection, as_of: date = None) -> list[dict]:
    if as_of is None:
        as_of = date.today()
    rows = db.execute("""        SELECT * FROM cost_review_actions
        WHERE status IN ('open', 'in_progress')
          AND due_date < ?
        ORDER BY due_date ASC
    """, (as_of.isoformat(),)).fetchall()
    return [dict(r) for r in rows]

def send_overdue_reminders(db: sqlite3.Connection, notify_fn) -> int:
    """Send Slack/email reminders for items due in 3 days or overdue.""""
    warning_date = (date.today() + timedelta(days=3)).isoformat()
    rows = db.execute("""        SELECT * FROM cost_review_actions
        WHERE status IN ('open', 'in_progress')
          AND due_date <= ?
        ORDER BY team, due_date
    """, (warning_date,)).fetchall()

    for row in rows:
        item = dict(row)
        is_overdue = item['due_date'] < date.today().isoformat()
        notify_fn(
            to=item['owner'],
            subject=f"[FinOps] {'OVERDUE' if is_overdue else 'DUE SOON'}: {item['title']}",
            body=(
                f"Action '{item['title']}' is {'overdue' if is_overdue else f'due {item["due_date"]}'}.
"
                f"Success metric: {item['success_metric']}
"
                f"Please update the status in the FinOps tracker."
            ),
        )
    return len(rows)
At the next review, completed actions are verified against the metric stated in the

field. Verification compares the baseline period (when the action was created) to the current period. This closes the loop and distinguishes real optimisations from changes that happened for unrelated reasons.

def verify_action_impact(
    db: sqlite3.Connection,
    action: CostReviewActionItem,
    baseline_period: str,
    current_period: str,
) -> dict:
    """
    After an action is closed, compare the relevant metric in the
    baseline period (when the action was created) vs current period.
    """"
    # Example: verify cost reduction for a specific feature
    baseline = db.execute("""        SELECT AVG(total_cost_usd) AS avg_cost, COUNT(*) AS n
        FROM conversation_costs
        WHERE team = ? AND period = ?
    """, (action.team, baseline_period)).fetchone()

    current = db.execute("""        SELECT AVG(total_cost_usd) AS avg_cost, COUNT(*) AS n
        FROM conversation_costs
        WHERE team = ? AND period = ?
    """, (action.team, current_period)).fetchone()

    baseline_cost = baseline["avg_cost"] or 0
    current_cost = current["avg_cost"] or 0
    delta = current_cost - baseline_cost
    n = current["n"] or 0

    return {
        "action_id": action.action_id,
        "baseline_period": baseline_period,
        "current_period": current_period,
        "baseline_avg_cost": baseline_cost,
        "current_avg_cost": current_cost,
        "delta_usd": delta,
        "delta_pct": delta / baseline_cost if baseline_cost else 0,
        "annualised_impact_usd": delta * n * 12,
        "goal_achieved": delta < 0,   # any reduction counts
    }

Move a team from monthly to quarterly reviews only when two consecutive quarters show: zero CRITICAL alerts, all MoM cost changes within ±15% of forecast, and fewer than two OVERDUE action items per quarter. Meeting cadence is a maturity signal, not a privilege to be granted.
: The first 15 minutes are spent interpreting the data instead of discussing it. Send the report 2 days before — every time.
: 'The team will investigate' guarantees nothing happens. Every action needs a named individual.
: 'Reduce cost' cannot be verified. 'Reduce cost_per_conv for support-bot below $0.04 by June 30' can.
: The first 5 minutes on previous actions is not optional overhead — it is the accountability mechanism. Removing it breaks the cycle.
: One overdue item per review is noise; three or more is a signal that the team is over-committed or the actions were too aggressive. Revisit scope, not just deadlines.
Integrating with the Budget Governance Process

If a review reveals that a team will exceed budget, the team lead must submit a budget adjustment request through the governance process (see Budget Enforcement Guardrails) before the review ends — not as a follow-up item. The FinOps partner brings the exception request template to every review meeting that shows budget utilisation above 85%.

A monthly 30-minute team FinOps review is the mechanism that converts cost visibility into cost discipline. The meeting succeeds when: the report arrives two days before, previous actions are reviewed first, every new action has a named owner and a measurable success metric, and completed actions are verified against their stated goal at the next review. The action item database and automated reminders enforce accountability between meetings. Teams that run this cycle consistently for two quarters achieve the baseline FinOps hygiene needed to shift to a quarterly cadence.
